title: Glioblastoma Multiforme -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: Due to the aggressive nature of the tumor, palliative care should be initiated as soon as the diagnosis is made. It is essential to determine the goals of care and patients' wishes throughout the continuum of care. Patients and family members must discuss prognosis to set expectations about the disease. The overall goal is to maintain the patient's quality of life as long as possible with adequate management of their symptomatology. Nevertheless, discussions regarding newer therapeutic avenues and clinical trials should be had in eligible patients. [67]

title: Astrocytoma -- Treatment / Management
content: Recommendations for recurrent tumors include reoperation, carmustine wafers, alternate chemotherapeutic regimens, and investigational IDH -targeted approaches. Re-radiation is rarely helpful; an additional 36 Gy in 18 fractions may be given. Bevacizumab, a humanized VEGF monoclonal antibody, has activity in recurrent glioblastoma, potentially increasing progression-free survival (PFS) and reducing peritumoral edema and glucocorticoid use. [12] IDH -targeted approaches, including vorasidenib and other small-molecule inhibitors of mutant IDH1 and 2 (ivosidenib, olutasidenib, safusedinib), are in trials. Vaccine strategies are also being explored. Recurrent glioblastoma treatment decisions must take into consideration factors such as previous therapy, time to relapse, performance status, and quality of life. Whenever possible, patients with recurrent disease should be enrolled in clinical trials.

title: Tumor Treatment Fields in Oncology -- Indications -- Glioblastoma
content: Glioblastoma is the most dangerous primary malignant brain tumor and has the worst prognosis. It is also the most common primary brain malignancy with an incidence rate of 3.2 to 4.5 per 100,000 individuals. [8] Despite significant advances in its treatment options, it still has a grim survival rate. Regardless of the treatment, recurrence in glioblastoma remains a major problem. Moreover, an effective treatment strategy for recurrent glioblastoma has not been found yet. [9]

title: Low-Grade Gliomas -- Medical Oncology
content: Targeted therapies such as BRAF/MEK inhibitors may be appropriate in certain gliomas, such as pilocytic astrocytomas, pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma (PXA), and ganglioneuromas. Patients that are incompletely resected or have progression with a BRAF V600E activating mutations may be eligible for Dabrafenib/trametinib or vemurafenib/cobimetinib. These drugs arrest cell growth by inhibiting B-Raf (dabrafenib and vemurafenib) and MEK (trametinib and cobimetinib), which are part of the Ras-Raf-MEK-ERK pathway that stimulates cell proliferation. For subependymal giant cell astrocytomas, an mTOR inhibitor such as everolimus may be considered.

title: Carcinomatous Meningitis -- Treatment / Management -- Systemic Chemotherapy
content: Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) tyrosine kinase inhibitors (EGFR-TKI) are used in lung cancers with EGFR mutations. [44] Erlotinib, gefitinib, and osimertinib are EGFR inhibitors. HER2 TKI is used in breast cancers. Lapatinib and tucatinib are examples. [45] BRAF inhibitors like vemurafenib have shown good clinical response and improved survival with leptomeningeal metastasis in patients with melanoma. [46] Anaplastic lymphoma kinase (ALK) inhibitors are used in lung cancers with ALK rearrangements. Drugs include crizotinib, ceritinib, alectinib, brigatinib, and lorlatinib. [47]

title: Leptomeningeal Carcinomatosis -- Medical Oncology -- Targeted Therapy
content: Bevacizumab (VGEF inhibitor) and dabrafenib (BRAF inhibitor) have been reported to demonstrate a response in LC from melanoma. IT trastuzumab in LC from HER-2 positive breast cancer also has shown some promise and a favorable adverse effect profile. There are several ongoing phase II trials on the subject. EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) has shown a response to erlotinib and gefitinib, but at higher doses, as they do not cross the BBB easily. There are ongoing trials of other tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) in LC from EGFR-mutant NSCLC that have shown promising results in terms of extended survival. Anaplastic lymphoma kinase (ALK) inhibitors are another class of drugs shown to be effective in LC from NSCLC with ALK rearrangements in ongoing trials.

title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
content: Blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) encompasses leukemias, including lymphoid (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid (ابيضاض الدم النقوي) forms, with specific subtypes such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Targeted kinase inhibition is a central therapeutic strategy. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—are employed, the first three for lymphoid leukemia and the latter three for myeloid leukemia. BTK inhibitors, namely ibrutinib and zanubrutinib, belong to the same protein‑kinase inhibitor class, with ibrutinib indicated for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein‑kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are specifically used in acute myeloid leukemia. Conventional cytotoxic and corticosteroid agents such as cytarabine, dexamethasone acetate, dexamethasone phosphate, dexamethasonisonikotinát, irinotecan hydrochloride, pipobroman, and prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate also have activity against various blood cancers. Dexamethasone acetate additionally serves in the management of septic shock. Conversely, viral agents such as human herpesvirus‑1 and the oncolytic therapy talimogene laherparepvec are contraindicated in patients with blood cancer. These interrelated disease classifications, drug classes, and contraindications illustrate the complexity of therapeutic decision‑making in hematologic malignancies.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
content: The community describes blood cancer (ابْيضَاضُ الدَّم) as a group of leukemic disorders comprising lymphoid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم اللمفاوي) and myeloid leukemia (ابيضاض الدم النقوي), which correspond to chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن) and acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), respectively. Targeted therapy relies heavily on protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. BCR‑ABL inhibitors—dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, imatinib mesylát, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib—block the BCR‑ABL tyrosine kinase; the former four are used for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride, and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase inhibitors ibrutinib and zanubrutinib also belong to this class, with ibrutinib approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Additional protein kinase inhibitors include EGFR inhibitors canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, as well as other ATC‑listed agents cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin; gilteritinib and midostaurin are employed in acute myeloid leukemia. Corticosteroids such as dexamethasone acetate (used for blood cancer and septic shock), dexamethasone phosphate, prednisolone sodium metasulphobenzoate, prednisolone sodium phosphate, and prednisolone tebutate demonstrate efficacy in hematologic malignancies. Chemotherapeutic agents irinotecan hydrochloride and pipobroman are also utilized. Contraindicated interventions include infection with human herpesvirus 1 and oncolytic viral therapy talimogene laherparepvec, which may exacerbate the disease. Collectively, these agents illustrate the role of kinase‑targeted and immunomodulatory drugs in the comprehensive management of leukemic disorders.

title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are classified as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Within this class, ibrutinib and zanubrutinib are members; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors also belong to the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are specific EGFR inhibitors. Other protein kinase inhibitors listed in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification include cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are indicated for the treatment of acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد), while cabozantinib is a member of this group with oncologic applications. Collectively, these agents illustrate how diverse protein kinase–targeting drugs—spanning BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other kinase inhibitors—are integrated into cancer therapy, each targeting specific malignancies and contributing to personalized patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 89
content: The compound 4‑aminopyrazolo(3,4‑d)pyrimidine is classified as an anticholesteremic agent, and within this anticholesteremic category a subcategory called hematologic agents (hematologické látky) is defined. Hematologic agents include drugs such as zanubrutinib, filgrastim, and protamines. Zanubrutinib, together with ibrutinib, belongs to the class of Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors; BTK inhibitors are themselves a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors that have antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity. Ibrutinib is indicated for the treatment of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن). Filgrastim, also a hematologic agent, is used to stimulate white‑blood‑cell production, and protamines represent another type of hematologic agent. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors constitute another group of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties. Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are examples of EGFR inhibitors. A broader grouping labeled “Other protein kinase inhibitors in ATC” also falls under the protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents class. This group includes cabozantinib, gilteritinib, and midostaurin. Gilteritinib and midostaurin have therapeutic activity against acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد). Overall, the taxonomy links several targeted therapies—BTK inhibitors, EGFR inhibitors, and other protein kinase inhibitors—to their respective clinical indications in hematologic malignancies such as chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myeloid leukemia, while also situating them within broader pharmacologic categories like anticholesteremic agents and protein kinase inhibitor classes.
